coredata.dc:title: Bandgap tailoring and enhancing the aromatization in cysteine-based carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Feghhi, Fazeleh
Minagar, Ava
Madaah Hosseini, H.R.
coredata.dc:description: Cysteine, as a non-aromatic precursor, was used to produce Nitrogen (N) and Sulfur (S) sources for preparing N, S-doped carbon dots (CDs) with tunable luminescence emission. Despite the tremendous investigations, the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs is still unclear due to its complex core–shell structure, variety of surface functional groups, and structure dependency. This study focuses on controlling aromatization and graphitization processes during the hydrothermal synthesis on CDs by using Citric Acid (CA) and Ammonium persulfate. Detailed characterizations by FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, and HR-TEM are provided to suggest both chemical and bandgap structures. Results reveal that the red-shift of PL occurred due to the graphitization and increasing content of graphitic nitrogen in the core, as well as the Pyridinic and Amine groups creating sub-bands on the surface. These findings resolve the controversy on the PL mechanism of Cysteine-based CDs and provide a general guide for increasing the aromatization and graphitization degree from non-aromatic precursors which clarify the mechanism exploration and structural analysis of other types of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bandgap tailoring
Cysteine
Luminescence mechanism
Structural analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectroscopy of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid (CD1), ammonium persulfate (CD2), sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1), ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2) (a), and Zeta potential values of the samples (b).

The expanded and deconvoluted XPS results for of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid (CD1) (a-d), sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1) (e-h), ammonium persulfate (CD2) (i-l), ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2) (m-p).
HR-TEM images of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1) and ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2) in two different magnifications.

Schematic view of the formation mechanisms and chemical structures of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid (CD1) (a), sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1) (b), ammonium persulfate (CD2) (c), ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2) (d).

Optical analysis of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid (CD1), sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1), ammonium persulfate (CD2), ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2): UV– Vis Absorption spectra of CD1/n-CD1 (a), and CD2 /n-CD2 (b), PL emission spectra of CD1 (c), CD2 (d), n-CD1 (e) and n-CD2 samples (f) at different excitation wavelengths. Comparative view of samples emission excited by 350 nm (g), and 450 nm (h).

Illustration of the proposed bandgap structure based on the Absorbance, Excitation, and Emission wavelengths of the cysteine-based CD samples synthesized with sulfuric acid (CD1) (a), ammonium persulfate (CD2) (b), sulfuric acid and CA (n-CD1) (c), ammonium persulfate and CA (n-CD2) (d).
Graphic abstract of effective structural factors in core and shell luminescent emission of the cysteine-based carbon dots.
Atomic percentage of C, O, N and S dopants in different CD samples, calculated by XPS survey scan.
A comparative survey for luminescence behavior of cysteine-based CDs A comparative survey for luminescence behavior of cysteine-based CDs.

head.ce:title: Bandgap tailoring and enhancing the aromatization in cysteine-based carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cysteine, as a non-aromatic precursor, was used to produce Nitrogen (N) and Sulfur (S) sources for preparing N, S-doped carbon dots (CDs) with tunable luminescence emission. Despite the tremendous investigations, the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs is still unclear due to its complex core–shell structure, variety of surface functional groups, and structure dependency. This study focuses on controlling aromatization and graphitization processes during the hydrothermal synthesis on CDs by using Citric Acid (CA) and Ammonium persulfate. Detailed characterizations by FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, and HR-TEM are provided to suggest both chemical and bandgap structures. Results reveal that the red-shift of PL occurred due to the graphitization and increasing content of graphitic nitrogen in the core, as well as the Pyridinic and Amine groups creating sub-bands on the surface. These findings resolve the controversy on the PL mechanism of Cysteine-based CDs and provide a general guide for increasing the aromatization and graphitization degree from non-aromatic precursors which clarify the mechanism exploration and structural analysis of other types of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, Carbon Dots (CDs) derived from natural precursors have garnered more attention in a wide range of applications, including biological labeling and bioimaging [1], optoelectronics [2], and biological and chemical sensing [3]. Tunable optoelectronic properties [4], photostability, high aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility are all advantages of CDs over semiconductor quantum dots [5].

Tunable luminescence in the visible light spectrum is the most interesting feature of CDs. Various studies have focused on the luminescence mechanism and bandgap tailoring to produce highly luminescent CDs. CDs generated from pure carbon mostly emit blue light in the 450 nm range. The primary key in bandgap tailoring is using dopants in the precursor. The chemical and physical characteristics and biological effects of CDs can be altered by adding heteroatoms of nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorous, fluorine, and other elements to a variety of carbon-containing precursor materials [6]. Among all dopants used to adjust the luminescence of CDs, N and S co-doped CDs are more adaptable, especially in terms of enhanced optoelectronic properties for sensors and photocatalysts [7,8].

A premium way to produce N, S co-doped CDs is using N, S-containing precursors. Cysteine is considered a conditionally essential amino acid and the main sulfur-rich component for many living organisms from plants to the human body which is used both in the surface treatment of CDs and as a precursor [9]. Although surface treatment by cysteine as biocompatible capping agent is suggested an efficient method for contrast enhancing in bio-imaging and antibacterial biofilms [10], Cysteine-derived CDs can provide luminescence tunability by adjusting the bandgap structure.

The presence of N and S in the structure of CDs can lead to bandgap tailoring and enhance luminescence emission. Structural factors such as graphitization degree, N atom positioning, functional groups, and surface states should be controlled precisely to achieve superior results and avoid self-bleaching [11]. Studies have revealed that controlling the mentioned parameters during the synthesis process is possible by adjusting the chemistry of the precursors, carbonizer acid, and solvent as well as surface modifications including passivation and activation processes [12,13].

The present study is focused on a deeper understanding of the relation between the chemistry of precursors, chemical and bandgap structures, and luminescence properties of synthesized CDs. To control the luminescence emission, the effect of two sulfate-based carbonizing agents has been studied by using a combination of cysteine or citric acid/cysteine as the precursor. Structures of the synthesized CDs were characterized by FTIR, XPS, HR-TEM techniques, and the band gap structure has been discussed based on the CDs' optical properties and chemical structure.

All chemicals, including l-cysteine (99%), sulfuric acid (37%), ammonium persulfate (98%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%) and ammonia (37%), were purchased from Merck and used without further purification. The photoluminescence emission of the samples was investigated using an Agilent G9800A spectrophotometer. Quantum yields (QY) were evaluated measuring the ratio between fluorescence and absorbance at a fixed wavelength comparing with a standard solution of 9,10-diphenyl anthracene [14]. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Visible) spectroscopy was performed using a Jenway 6705 UV/Vis spectrophotometer; samples were prepared in DI water. Fourier transformation of infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained using a Bruker Vertex spectrometer; samples were dried on heater at 80 °C and dispersed in KBr powder and pressed to form a tablet. The Zeta potential was recorded at neutral pH on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano S instrument. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using an HR-TEM FEI (TEC9G20) operating at 200 keV; samples were prepared by drying carbon dot solution on the TEM grid under an infrared lamp. Detailed information about the crystal structure was provided by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS wide scan was analyzed from 0 to 1200 eV, and the analysis of the results of the measurements was conducted by a hemispherical analyzer with an ESCA/AES system (CHA, Specs model EA10 plus); samples were dried on heater at 80 °C.

NS doped CDs were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal process using a Teflon-lined Autoclave (30 ml) at 200 °C for 6 h. Sulfate ion has been reported as a suitable carbonizing agent in many previous studies [15]. Therefore, H2SO4 and (NH4)2S2O8 were used in CD1 and CD2 samples, respectively. CD1 was synthesized with 0.1 g l-cysteine dispersed in 10 ml deionized water as a precursor, 0.27 mol.l−1 H2SO4 as a strong carbonizing agent, and a proper amount of NH3 to adjust the final product’s pH to 7. Since pH is an effective parameter on CDs' luminescence emission, it is vital to adjust the synthesis pH to 7 [16]. CD2 was synthesized with the same amount of l-cysteine solution and 20 mg.l−1 (NH4)2S2O8 as the carbonizing agent, and the pH was adjusted to 7 using the proper amount of NH3. To study the effect of CA, n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples were synthesized by adding 0.1 g CA to CD1 and CD2 protocols. Synthesized samples were light yellow clear solutions with no heavy particles or sedimentation; therefore, no further purification was performed.

Formation of the CDs is mainly considered to consist of the following steps: precursor degradation and aggregation of small organic molecules, formation of a dense core, and aromatization and carbonization of the core; then, the process could be followed by the core growth and graphitization. As studies reported, a strong acid could facilitate the carbonization process and affect the graphitization degree [15]; therefore, sulfuric acid was used as a carbonizing agent in CD1. It is noteworthy that focusing on the cysteine structure leads us to enrich the reaction with nitrogen and start SS chaining reactions by replacing sulfuric acid with ammonium persulfate in CD2. Ammonium persulfate dissolving reaction produces H2O2; The produced H2O2 can work as a catalyst to change the formation mechanism of the CDs [17].

On the other hand, CA is the most common source for synthesizing CDs. Numerous studies have reported nitrogen-doped carbon dots using CA as the main precursor and a nitrogen-rich molecule as the nitrogen source. Results reveal that CA has a significant effect on the structure and luminescence properties of CDs by determining the C/N atomic ratio and changing the position of nitrogen atoms in the structure by influencing the aromatization and graphitization degree [16]. To have a deeper understanding of CDs’ formation and luminescence behavior, the effect of CA in the precursor is studied in n-CD1 and n-CD2.

Finding a bridge between the chemical structure of the CDs and their optical behavior could be inferred by the characterization results, including FTIR, Zeta potential and XPS spectroscopies along with the optical properties investigated by UV–Vis and PL studies. To achieve this objective, first, a probable formation mechanism for each sample is discussed by focusing on the effect of carbonizers and the presence of CA in the samples. For a better understanding of the luminescence mechanism, a modified bandgap structure has been discussed in the optical properties section.

The functional groups of the CDs could be determined by FTIR spectrum of samples (Fig. 1 (a)). As shown in Fig. 1, FTIR spectra in all samples are very similar except for the slight changes in some oxygen and nitrogen bonds in 1000–1600 cm−1 region.

Peaks between 2800 and 3500 cm−1 can be attributed to OH and NH bonds indicating hydroxyl and amine groups on the CDs' surface, which contribute to the highly hydrophilic nature of the CDs. The vibration peak at 2050 cm−1 is related to CN bonds in graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen groups. The vibration at 1720 cm−1 in samples with CA (n-CD1 and n-CD2) is ascribed to the CN bond in graphitic nitrogen[18]. CC typical stretches at 1570 and 1670 are enhanced in samples with CA (n-CD1 and n-CD2), which describe more aromatic rings in these samples. The distinct absorption band at 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to CO and CN, respectively [19,20]. The vibration peak at 1200 in CD2 and n-CD2 is typically reported for CO bond stretch, and the 1090 cm−1 peak belongs to CS bond at the surface, while the strong peak at 615 is assigned to CH bonds in the samples [21]. Zeta potential values at pH 7, are demonstrated in Fig. 7 (b) which indicate an increasing negative surface charge in samples with CA; from −17 mV in CD1 to −20 mV in n-CD1 and from −18 mV in CD2 to −25 mV in n-CD2. This trend matches with the increasing amount of negatively charged carboxyl groups as seen in atomic percent of the samples calculated from XPS survey spectra in Table 1 .

As observed in FTIR results, the difference between samples with and without CA results from the change in position of nitrogen atoms in the structures and the variation of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups. XPS results of the samples shown in Fig. 2 show the position of dopants, functional groups, and dopants concentration in the structure of the CDs.

The photoluminescence behavior of Carbon Dots is dependent not only on the number of dopants but also on the position of dopants in the chemical structure. As previous theoretical and experimental studies suggest [22], the presence of graphitic nitrogen in a CD structure can induce a red shift in absorption spectra by decreasing the bandgap energy of the structure. In addition, despite other nitrogen groups such as pyrrole, pyridine, and amino, graphitic nitrogen enhances the broader luminescence of CDs. Therefore, a precise understanding of nitrogen bonds can lead to a better understanding of the mechanism of photoluminescence.

Fig. 2 represents C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p spectra for CD1, n-CD1, CD2, and n-CD2 specimens, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (i), CD1 and CD2 samples have similar peaks on 284.7 and 287.2 eV attributed to the graphitic sp2 hybridization of carbon and CO bonding in oxygen-containing functional groups, respectively [23]. As shown in Fig. 2 (e) and (m), the presence of CA has changed the structure of the carbonic cores in n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples. The n-CD1 spectrum represents peaks at 285.9 eV corresponding to CN/CS, 284 and 288.2 eV corresponding to CO and CO/CN bonds, respectively. Fig. 2 (m) indicates carbon graphitic sp2 bond hybridization at 284.8 eV and the shoulder at 288.2 eV belonging to CO/CN bonds [24].

While CD1 and CD2 showed to have CO and CO bonds at 531 and 532 eV respectively, the O1s spectrum reveals that n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples showed significantly more out-of-plane COC bonds at 531.5 and 532.8 eV, indicating the presence of more surface functional groups [25].

The N1s spectrum confirms the presence of nitrogen in the core of CA-containing CDs. In CD1 and CD2 samples, the peaks at 399 eV and 401 eV are assigned to the CN bonds and amino group or pyrrolic groups, respectively, which prove functionalization at the edges of the CDs. Accordingly, the 401.6 eV peaks in n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples indicate the presence of graphitic N, and the peak at 399 eV is assigned to the pyridinic nitrogen in the chemical structure [26]. Doping c-dots with graphitic nitrogen results in lower bandgap energy and increases the photoluminescence range of emission and photocatalytic activity of the CDs in near-IR spectra. Studying the S2p spectrum reveals that all samples have CS bonds at 162–164.5 eV and CSC at 165–169 eV. Since sulfur is mainly a surface modifier for CDs, different CS, CSC, and C-SOx-C bonds are variations of sulfur and oxygen-containing surface functional groups [27].

The atomic percentages of elements calculated from XPS peaks are presented in Table 1. Atomic ratios were calculated by dividing the peak area for each element from XPS survey scan to the photoionization coefficient. Then atomic percent composition of each element is determined by dividing the number of atoms by the total number of atoms and multiplying by 100. The calculation error is up to 1 At%. These results show that nitrogen and sulfur are almost constant in all samples, but there is a remarkable change in oxygen contents. It could be seen that CA has increased the atomic percent of oxygen from 20 to 23 in n-CD1 and from 20 to 31 in n-CD2 samples, respectively.

The results of HR-TEM examinations of the CDs in Fig. 3 show monodispersed uniform spherical morphology of CDs. Both n-CD1 and n-CD2 have a size distribution between 3 and 7 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 5 nm. These results indicate that the carbonization process by sulfuric acid and ammonium persulfate is probably confined to 5–7 nm particle diameters. The same particle size shows that differences in luminescence are size-independent and probably due to different doping or carbonization degrees. The corresponding ring patterns of both samples represent the amorphous nature of the CDs, and the lattice fringe was not detectable by HR-TEM (Fig. 3).

According to the literature, the general CD formation mechanism involves the following steps: precursor degradation, dehydration reactions which form small organic molecules, aromatization, and condensation to form the luminescent core [28]. Then, depending on the synthesis condition, the process could be followed by the core growth or surface functionalization. Based on the present work’s findings, and the formation of CDs from cysteine in H2SO4 and (NH4)2S2O8 are from different pathways. Although it is noticeable that CA may change the final structure of the CDs at the end. Accordingly, a formation path is suggested for each sample in Fig. 4 .

In CD1 sample, the amine group can facilitate the dehydrolysis reaction. In a strongly acidic solution, cysteine molecules self-assemble into a spherical structure and dehydrolyze to form a first carbonic core. As observed in XPS results, the amine groups can react with neighbor carboxylic groups and form pyrrolic N through intramolecular dehydrolysis reactions. Pyrrolic N indicates that N bonds with C in 5 member-ring structures formed from dehydrolysis between neighbor carboxyl and amide groups. Condensation of carbon atoms is assumed to be confined since the lattice fringe was not detectable by HR-TEM (Fig. 3).

To study the forming process of CD2 sample, it is vital to consider the effect of ammonium persulfate as a catalyst to replace sulfuric acid. Ammonium persulfate can hydrolyze in water into ammonium hydrogen sulfate and hydrogen peroxide. Further ammonium hydrogen sulfate decomposes into sulfate ions. The produced hydrogen peroxide decomposes to H2O in an aqueous solution releasing an electron; then facilitates cysteine polymerization process. As discussed in CD2 and n-CD2 XPS results, CSC bonds in these samples show that sulfur atoms are involved in the carbon rings as well as the surface groups. XPS results also revealed that CD2 includes pyridinic and amine groups. Since the ratio of C and N bonds has not changed between CD1 and CD2 samples (Table 1), a slight redshift in the luminescence of CD2 sample is expected.

To discuss the n-CD1 and n-CD2 structures, it should be considered that adding CA as a precursor affects the N/C atomic ratio and controls the fusion and carbonization processes. Intermediate structures from CA and l-cysteine form in the aromatization state. These intermediate structures are amide and hydrazine-containing molecules with luminescent centers [29]. In n-CD1 sample, after the formation of intermediate molecules, nitrogen and hydroxyl groups facilitate dehydrolysis reactions and formation of graphitic N during the aromatization stage. According to XPS results for CD1 and n-CD1 Fig. 2 (b, f), the number of graphitic N is significantly more than pyridinic N. In addition, other types of N state are not observed in the XPS, which proves that N atoms entered the carbon framework through dehydrolysis and aromatization stage. Graphitic N enhances luminescence, while pyridinic N makes a red shift in the luminescence emission of the CDs. In n-CD2 sample, CA-cysteine intermediate molecules provide pyridinic and graphitic N. Besides, more oxygen surface groups are observed in XPS results for n-CD2 sample (Fig. 2 (o)). Oxygen surface states affect luminescence of carbon dots by decreasing bandgap and moving PL spectra to higher wavelengths [30].

The synthesized samples were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy and PL spectroscopy to evaluate the photophysical properties of the carbon dots. Also, the Quantum yields of the CDs were measured at 400 nm excitation for all samples. Based on the absorbance characteristics of samples shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), CD1 and CD2 samples have similar absorbance prominent peaks at 250 nm and a second absorbance peak at 282 nm for CD1 and 264 nm for CD2. These peaks corresponded to 4.96 eV, 4.40 eV, and 4.70 eV transitions, respectively. However, absorbance peaks of n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples are moved to 323 nm, which is assigned to the 3.85 eV transition. In other words, in n-CD1 and n-CD2, compared to CD1 and CD2 samples, the electron transition path decreased from 4.96 to 3.85 eV.

The emission characteristics of the CDs are presented in Fig. 5 (b-h). As shown in Fig. 5 (c-f), the luminescence of the synthesized CDs is excitation-dependent. Fig. 5 (c) demonstrates that CD1 sample had a strong luminescence peak at q wavelength of 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which is attributed to the 2.82 eV transition; moreover, as the excitation wavelength increases, the luminescence drops significantly. Quantum yield for CD1 calculated 24.2% at 400 nm excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5 (d), the luminescence emission of CD2 sample is not considerable, and the QY is 2.2%. This result represents that ammonium persulfate is not a proper carbonizing agent compared to create luminescent cores from pure cysteine.

Using CA significantly affected the luminescence in the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths, as well as the emission brightness and QY. As shown in Fig. 5 (e, f), the n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples show the optimum emission at 531 nm and 504 nm, excited by 500 nm and 450 nm, corresponding to 2.33 eV and 2.46 eV transitions, respectively. The calculated QY is 25.6% and 30.4% for n-CD1 and n-CD2 respectively. A comparison of the optical responses of the CDs with and without CA in different excitation wavelengths is presented in Fig. 5 (g) and (h). Enhanced aromatization in CDs with CA effects the optical behavior of the CDs and creates about 100 nm redshift in excite and emission wavelengths. According to Fig. 5 (g), samples without CA (CD1 and CD2) represent the most luminescence emission in 440 nm when excited by 350 nm wavelength. While, samples with CA (n-CD1 and n-CD2) show the most luminescence emissions at 500 and 530 nm when excited by 450 nm wavelength, respectively (Fig. 5 (h)). Although adding CA slightly improved the quantum yield from 24.2% to 25.6% in CD1 and n-CD1, in CD2 and n-CD2 the quantum yield is enhanced significantly from 2.2% to 30.4%. It is worthy to be noted that while luminescent cores could not form pure cysteine by ammonium persulfate, CA can facilitate the aromatization to form more luminescent cores. This comparison clarifies the impact of enhanced aromatization in samples with CA on the bandgap structure and luminescence emission of the CDs.

The luminescent behavior of the synthesized CDs suggests that the luminescence mechanism has changed as a function of two key factors: bandgap value, and electron transition pathways, including sub-bands and trap levels of energy. Numerous studies have worked to clarify the relationship between CD chemical structures and luminescence behavior. These studies suggest that size and graphitization degree of the luminescent core, doping agents, and surface functional groups are among the most effective parameters. In this work, we have tried to give a unified picture of the most probable luminescent mechanism for the synthesized CDs based on observed structures.

On one hand, when it comes to the carbonic core, research results have indicated that not only the size of CDs can affect the bandgap, but also the degree of graphitization changes the luminescence behavior both in wavelength and intensity [31]. In the present study, HR-TEM images show no significant change in the size of different CD samples, while the XPS results (Fig. 2 (f, n)) proved the presence of graphitic N in n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples as a sign of more graphitization of the core. Fig. 6 presents the schematic model for the bandgap structure of the samples in which gray arrows represent the main bandgap calculated from UV–Vis results and blue and red arrows show sub-bands from PL results. All bandgap energies were estimated by converting UV–Vis absorption edges and PL excitation and emission wavelengths into energy using E = hc/λ, where c is the speed of light and h is the Plank’s constant. This approximation error is up to 0.02 eV, which can be neglected since we are trying to trace the changes in different samples. Theoretical calculations on CDs with similar sizes suggest that graphitic N can reduce the bandgap by about 1 eV [11]. This result matches perfectly with the absorption peak shift in n-CD1 and n-CD2 samples from 4.96 eV to 3.84 eV, in which graphitic nitrogen is evidenced in chemical structures.

In Fig. 5 (c-f), all samples showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission which mainly corresponds to surface states, specifically oxygen groups. Therefore, studying surface states could reveal the main source of change in luminescence behavior.

Theoretical calculations predict that about 0.3 eV bandgap reduction can occur by creating each sub-band corresponds to the pyridinic, pyrrolic, and amine nitrogen groups. Specifically, luminescence emission was enhanced by nitrogen substitution (pyridinic/pyrrolic nitrogen) and also surface amino groups which are reported to trap surface electrons for more efficient radiative recombination and hence higher quantum yields. The PL emission derived from the surface states is controlled by nitrogen functional groups, and potentially, a selection of appropriate nitrogen-containing molecules can lead to tailored PL and result in single wavelength emitting CDs [32].

The Oxygen functional groups have a more significant effect on lowering LUMO energy level, resulting in the bandgap narrowing for up to 0.5 eV and a noticeable redshift of the PL peak. This is probably the main reason for the lowered LUMO band in CD1 and CD2 samples.

It should be mentioned that in presence of enough luminescent cores, the electronic structure is a function of dopants in CDs. N, S-dopants lead to more electronic states as well as more energy traps and recombination centers. In an overall view, QY increases by promoting radiative transitions and suppressing nonradiative transitions. Electron donating species in the structure usually improve the emissive transitions [32]. Therefore, oxygen containing groups, as electron donating species, enhance the QY. As mentioned in XPS results in Table 1 and Zeta potential measurements in Fig. 1, n-CD2 has more oxygen groups comparing to other samples, which could explain more negative charge and higher QY in n-CD2.

Table 2 presents a comparative survey for luminescence behavior based on published studies for the synthesized CDs using cysteine as a precursor. Although there are few works on making CDs from cysteine as a precursor, as shown in Table 2, quantum dots are prepared mostly by hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave techniques.

All the CDs prepared from cysteine had UV–Vis absorption peaks around 279 and 350 nm, and a photoluminescent emission between 415 and 460 nm with excitation around 350 to 420 nm. It is worth mentioning that QY measurement for cysteine-based carbon dots is reported from 15% to 41% in most of the studies mainly caused by radiative transitions between surface states. Although the photoluminescent mechanism is not discussed in many of the research studies, some reports have discussed the reasons for the excitation dependency or independency of photoluminescent emission. Liu et al. discussed that excitation-independent emission of the synthesized cysteine and polyethyleneimine CDs is due to the monotonous localization of surface states and narrow size distribution of n, s-CDs [41].

Li et al. used a hydrothermal method to synthesize L (or D) cysteine-based green light-emitting CDs. They reported that the CDs’ emission could results from the surface energy traps due to the presence of many carbonyl and amino groups on the surface and nitrogen and sulfur atoms inside the CDs [34]. In another report from CDs synthesized by L (or D) cysteine and sodium hydroxide, aggregation of CDs mentioned as a probable cause of redshift in emission by increasing the excitation wavelength [42].

Most researchers tried to enhance the luminescence emission of CDs by using other additives. Zheng et al. used l-cysteine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. They found based on PL excitation spectra that absorption bands from n-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds were the main source of emission [36]. Moreover, Guo et al. declared the same assertion based on their UV–Vis spectra results [40]The contribution of both quantum size effect and surface defects was mentioned as the most important factors for the luminescence behavior of their GQDs prepared from l-cysteine [37]. For the CDs synthesized from l-cysteine and P2O5 as precursors, it has been reported that the size-dependent optical properties, different maximum excitation and emission wavelength, and different fluorophore within the particles cause the fluorescence behavior of CDs [38]. For the microwave-assisted synthesized CDs from CA, l-cysteine, and dextrin, the quantum confinement effects and size-dependent optical properties of CDs are introduced as reasons for the excitation-dependent emission property of CDs [39].

Taking everything into account, the luminescence behavior of the cysteine CDs is assumed to be divisible into core and shell emissions (Fig. 7 ). As discussed above, the core luminescence wavelength is significantly affected by the N content, which has been studied by N/C ratio and presence of graphitic N as well as size and graphitization degree. O content in the core can also affect luminescence emission through CO bonds. On the other hand, surface states in the shell can change luminescence emission by creating sub-bands and electron traps. Surface states in cysteine CDs can be tailored by N functional groups such as amine, pyridinic, and pyrrolic N groups and surface passivation and activation caused by secondary treatment or different pH levels of the solution.

Cysteine-based CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal reaction of l-cysteine with sulfuric acid or ammonium persulfate as a carbonizer agent, with and without CA. Based on the characterization results, the formation mechanism, structural model, and bandgap structure of each CD system were discussed. XPS analysis revealed that the conjugated domains through aromatization between cysteine and CA facilitated the formation of graphitic N in the core of the CDs. Moreover, CA enhanced aromatization process in CDs formation from cysteine as a nonaromatic precursor, which effects bandgap structure and luminescence wavelength. Besides that, pyridinic, pyrrolic, and amine groups on the surface of the CDs created sub-bands and lowered the bandgap to 3.84 eV, emitting yellow-green light at 531 nm. Further investigations indicated that H2O2 produced by ammonium persulfate enhanced aromatization and facilitated the formation of graphitic N and O bonds in the core of the CDs, which affected the wavelength and intensity of emitted light. This work provided detailed information on the fluorescence mechanism and impact of CA in chemical and bandgap structure of the cysteine-based CDs to clear synthesis pathway for CDs in imaging, sensing, and catalytic applications.

Fazeleh Feghhi: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Ava Minagar: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. H.R. Madaah Hosseini: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All fundings are provided by Sharif University of Technology.
